metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the proposed `resolve_assignee` return type to a 3-tuple `tuple[Any, bool, bool]` (or a named dataclass) that carries the `authoritative` flag alongside the vendor value and `is_account_id` flag, preserving the gate at `outbound_fields.py:491` that prevents non-authoritative resolver results from emitting or suppressing assignee mutations.
evidence: Plan: 'OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool]'
outbound_fields.py:483-490: `_res = assignee_resolver(local_val, jira_key); acct, authoritative = _res[0], _res[1]; is_account_id = _res[2] if len(_res) > 2 else False`
outbound_fields.py:491: `if authoritative:` — the entire emit block is gated on authoritative
outbound_fields.py:497-507: unmappable→unassign is inside `if authoritative:` block
Plan: 'the 9b94 unmappable→unassign soft-fail... [is] byte-identical downstream' — unverifiable with 2-tuple return
findings.finding: The proposed `OutboundMapper.resolve_assignee` signature returns `tuple[Any, bool]` (vendor payload value + is-account-id flag), but the existing resolver it replaces returns a 3-tuple `(account_id, authoritative, is_account_id)`. The `authoritative` flag is load-bearing: when `authoritative=False` (no client, transient error), the current code falls through to legacy string-match behavior rather than emitting anything — verified at `outbound_fields.py:488-491`. The plan's 2-tuple drops this third element entirely, leaving no way for `resolve_assignee` to signal 'I cannot resolve — preserve legacy behavior' vs 'I resolved to unassigned'. The unmappable→unassign soft-fail (bug 9b94) and the identity fast-path both depend on `authoritative=True` gating entry to the emit branch.
findings.location: Approach §4b / _backend.py OutboundMapper protocol
scenarios: When the assignee resolver cannot reach Jira (no client or transient error), resolve_assignee cannot signal non-authoritative, so the neutral differ will either always emit or always suppress the assignee field — breaking the legacy string-match fallback.
The unmappable→unassign soft-fail path (currently guarded by `authoritative=True`) will misfire or be suppressed when the resolver cannot confirm unmappability.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the protocol to `resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool, bool]` where the three elements are `(vendor_assignee_value, authoritative, is_account_id)`, mirroring the existing 3-tuple contract of `_resolve_assignee_account_id`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the plan's scope boundary for labels and links: either (a) extend the scope to canonicalize `_diff_labels` and `_diff_links` inputs (e.g. pass `inbound_mapped_fields.get('labels')` and a canonical link representation), or (b) explicitly narrow the ADR 0035 §(d) amendment to 'scalar field comparison' only, and note labels/links as a follow-on gap, so the principle matches what is actually implemented.
evidence: Plan states goal: 'vendor shapes cross core only as opaque payloads produced/consumed by port calls at the boundaries'
outbound_differ.py:755: `label_mutations = _diff_labels(ticket, jira_fields, intent_set)` — raw jira_fields passed
outbound_differ.py:767: `link_mutations = _diff_links(ticket, jira_fields, binding_store)` — raw jira_fields passed
outbound_links.py:_existing_jira_links reads 'issuelinks', 'inwardIssue', 'outwardIssue' from raw jira_fields
outbound_differ.py:333: `jira_fields.get('labels')` inside _diff_labels (a core function)
Plan scope section lists outbound_differ.py and outbound_links.py is absent — labels/links not in scope
ADR 0035 §(d) amendment (§9) pins a principle the implementation does not fully satisfy
findings.finding: The plan's canonicalization is partial: `_diff_labels` (outbound_differ.py:755) and `_diff_link_mutations` (outbound_differ.py:767 via `_diff_links` in outbound_links.py) still receive the raw vendor `jira_fields` dict after this plan lands. `_diff_labels` reads `jira_fields.get('labels')` and `_diff_links` reads raw `issuelinks`/`inwardIssue`/`outwardIssue` through `_existing_jira_links`. The plan's ADR 0035 §(d) amendment pins the principle 'core compares only canonical shapes' — but this principle is overclaimed because the labels and links comparison paths in core still consume vendor-shaped dicts. AC2's denylist only guards `outbound_differ.py` itself; the vendor literals live in `outbound_links.py`, so the AC passes while the violation persists.
findings.location: Approach §2 / Scope section / AC2 / outbound_differ.py:755,767 / outbound_links.py:_existing_jira_links
scenarios: ADR 0035 §(d) as amended records 'core compares only canonical shapes' but a GitHub backend's inbound_mapper would still need to produce a Jira-shaped labels list and issuelinks dict for _diff_labels/_diff_links to work — the port pressure the plan was meant to relieve.
The AC2 denylist test will pass (vendor key literals are in outbound_links.py not outbound_differ.py), giving false confidence that the principle is fully implemented.

findings.finding: The managed-parent-clear gate `_parent_clear_is_managed` (adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py:311, called at :547) implements logic currently inside `_diff_fields`. The plan replaces the parent diff with canonical comparison (Approach §4: 'replacing outbound_fields.py:531-558'), but does not enumerate where the managed-parent-clear logic migrates or what its neutral-core equivalent looks like. Without this, the parent-clear managed-ref semantics are silently dropped.
findings.location: Plan Approach §4 ('replacing outbound_fields.py:531-558') / src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py:311,547

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism by which `outbound_mapper.map_local_to_remote` is 'restricted to changed fields': e.g., call `map_local_to_remote(ticket, ...)` and intersect the result dict with the set of vendor keys corresponding to changed local keys (using a local→vendor name table), or call it with a filtered ticket dict containing only changed fields, or add an explicit `fields` parameter to the method — and confirm the chosen mechanism is reflected in the OutboundMapper Protocol definition in `_backend.py`.
evidence: Plan §3: 'the vendor update payload is built at the emission boundary by the already-injected outbound_mapper.map_local_to_remote restricted to the changed fields'
_backend.py:87-93: OutboundMapper.map_local_to_remote takes (ticket, binding_store, local_ticket_types, emit_detach_clear) — a full ticket, no field-subset parameter
Plan does not specify how 'restricted to the changed fields' is mechanically achieved
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the changed-field vendor payload is built via `outbound_mapper.map_local_to_remote` 'restricted to the changed fields', but the current `OutboundMapper.map_local_to_remote` signature takes a full ticket dict (not a field subset), so the restriction mechanism is unspecified. The plan does not say whether a new method is added to the port, whether the caller calls `map_local_to_remote(ticket)` and then filters the result dict by changed local field names, or whether some other interface is used. This ambiguity could lead to implementations that either (a) emit the full mapped dict for every update (breaking the 'only changed fields' guarantee), or (b) add a new port method requiring every backend to implement it.
findings.location: Approach §3 / _backend.py OutboundMapper Protocol
scenarios: Implementer calls map_local_to_remote(ticket) to get the full vendor dict, then tries to filter it by changed local field names — but local field names (e.g. 'title') differ from vendor field names (e.g. 'summary'), so naive key filtering silently drops changes.
Implementer adds a new OutboundMapper method — this widens the port contract and requires updating the JiraBackend delegate and any test fakes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the new characterization test file explicitly in AC3's proving command (e.g. `pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_outbound_differ_canonical.py`) so the AC is deterministically verifiable.
evidence: AC3 text: 'verified by `pytest` on the new test file' — 'the new test file' has no name
Scope lists 'tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ (new characterization suite)' without a filename
findings.finding: AC3 ('The characterization suite proving old-vs-new decision equivalence … is green — verified by `pytest` on the new test file') does not name the new test file. The scope section says only 'tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ (new characterization suite; migrated re-export tests)', leaving the filename unspecified. A reviewer checking the AC cannot confirm which file to run, and an executor could satisfy the AC by adding the cases to an unrelated file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 / Scope bullet 'tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/'
scenarios: A reviewer cannot deterministically confirm AC3 is satisfied by inspecting a named artifact.
The proving command `pytest <unnamed file>` cannot be run as written without first searching the directory.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'verified by `pytest` on the new test file' in AC3 with 'verified by `pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_outbound_differ_characterization.py`' (or whichever filename is chosen) and add that filename to the scope bullet.

findings.finding: The `dropped_field_sink` (acd0) observability signal for `issuetype` is silently lost in the new flow. Today `_diff_fields` iterates over the local→Jira mapped fields, hits `issuetype`, skips emitting it, but still calls `_extract_jira_field(jira_fields, 'issuetype')` to detect and report a divergence to the sink. Under the new plan, the comparison loop iterates over canonical local fields; `issuetype` is excluded on update (step 6) before comparison, so no Jira-side issuetype value is ever read and the sink is never appended. The plan says 'issuetype excluded on update (today outbound_fields.py:449-459)' but does not state how the dropped-field-sink path for issuetype will be preserved.
findings.location: Approach step 6 (issuetype excluded on update); Scope: outbound_differ.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate in the plan (or Scope/Testing section) all test files that call `differ._diff_fields`, `differ._extract_jira_field`, `differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` via the `outbound_differ` module and specify whether each will be re-pointed to `adapters.jira.outbound_fields` or removed (at least: `test_diff_assignee_permissive.py`, `test_inbound_field_sync_directionality.py`, `test_baseline_arbitration.py`, `test_outbound_differ_adf_extraction.py`, `test_outbound_field_conflict_and_drop_alerts.py`, `test_diff_assignee_unmappable_converges.py`, `test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py`).
evidence: tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_diff_assignee_permissive.py:78,88,96,104,113,121,131 calls `differ._diff_fields(ticket, jira)` where differ is loaded from outbound_differ.py
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_inbound_field_sync_directionality.py:80,88,97,110,117,129 calls `differ._diff_fields(...)`
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_baseline_arbitration.py:96,121,136,152,172,188,202 calls `differ._diff_fields(...)`
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_outbound_differ_adf_extraction.py:59,68,97,106,124 calls `differ._extract_jira_field(...)` and `differ._diff_fields(...)`
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_outbound_field_conflict_and_drop_alerts.py:67,81,94,109,121 calls `differ._diff_fields(...)`
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_diff_assignee_unmappable_converges.py:80,91,98,106,116 calls `differ._diff_fields(...)`
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85 calls `outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`
outbound_differ.py:39-44 — the re-export block that step 8 proposes to delete
findings.finding: Multiple existing test files call `differ._diff_fields(...)`, `differ._extract_jira_field(...)`, and `differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` on the `outbound_differ` module object loaded by file path — these work today because `outbound_differ.py:39-44` re-exports those symbols from `adapters.jira.outbound_fields`. Step 8 deletes those imports, removing the re-exports. The plan says 're-export tests migrate to `adapters.jira.outbound_fields`' but does not enumerate the specific test files requiring updates, and the plan's scope section omits these test files.
findings.location: Approach step 8; Scope section (testing directory)
scenarios: After step 8 deletes the `from adapters.jira.outbound_fields import _diff_fields, _extract_jira_field, _LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE, _assignee_matches` block from `outbound_differ.py`, any test that loads `outbound_differ` by file path and accesses `differ._diff_fields` will get an AttributeError.

`make test` will fail on at least 6 existing test files (≥30 test functions) unless each is updated to load from `adapters.jira.outbound_fields` directly or the plan is amended to enumerate and migrate them.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope/Testing section, add: 'The following test files call `differ._diff_fields`/`_extract_jira_field`/`_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` via the `outbound_differ` re-export and must be updated to load from `adapters.jira.outbound_fields` directly: [list]. Alternatively, keep the re-export as a deprecated shim and add a deprecation comment, removing it in a follow-up.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the canonical key name (and confirm plain-text format) the neutral comparison helper reads for description from `map_remote_to_local` output in step 5 (e.g. add `description_text: str|None` to the documented canonical outputs, mirroring the explicit naming of `assignee_identity`/`remote_parent_id`).
evidence: "compare the port-sanitized local text (`FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description`) against the mapper-decoded remote text" — key name absent
Step 4 names `assignee_identity`, `remote_parent_id`, `reporter_identity` explicitly; no equivalent canonical key is named for description
AC2 denylist does not include `"description"`, so the key is not indirectly pinned there either
findings.finding: The canonical output key name for `description` in `map_remote_to_local` is never stated in the plan. Step 5 says 'compare the port-sanitized local text against the mapper-decoded remote text' but does not name the canonical dict key the neutral comparison helper must read, nor confirm the mapper emits plain text (not ADF) for that key. The three NEW canonical keys added by step 4 are explicitly named and typed, but description is handled implicitly.
findings.location: Approach step 5 / Testing bullet 'ADF description fit+rstrip'
scenarios: Executor must choose a canonical description key name (e.g. `description`, `description_text`, `body`) — if the existing inbound mapper already emits a key under a different name, the neutral helper and the mapper will silently diverge.
Characterization test for ADF description must assert on a specific key name — without it stated in the plan, the test could pass against the wrong key.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to step 4 or step 5: '`map_remote_to_local` emits `description_text: str|None` — the ADF-decoded, rstripped plain text — matching the key the neutral helper compares against `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description(local.description)`.' Confirm whether this key already exists in the Jira inbound mapper output, and if so name it explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the Scope path for the Jira inbound mapper file to `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/inbound_fields.py` so it matches the Approach references and the ADR 0035 adapter boundary.
evidence: Scope lists: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py (Jira mapper: aliases, parent remote id, reporter aliases)`
Approach step 4 says: `translation only, implemented in the vendor mapper inbound_fields.py:141-184 / delegate adapters/jira/backend.py:43-47`
ADR 0035 seam principle requires vendor-specific mapping to live under `adapters/jira/`, not in core `rebar_reconciler/`
findings.finding: The Scope entry for the Jira inbound mapper file is listed as `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py` (a core-layer path), but the Approach consistently refers to it as the vendor mapper file at `adapters/jira/inbound_fields.py:141-184` with delegate at `adapters/jira/backend.py:43-47`. This path mismatch will cause implementers to edit the wrong file (core instead of adapter) or create a new file at the wrong location.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py`; Approach step 4 — `inbound_fields.py:141-184`
scenarios: Implementer edits `rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py` (core) instead of `adapters/jira/inbound_fields.py`, violating the ADR 0035 seam and re-introducing the vendor-shape-in-core defect the plan is meant to fix.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Scope line to: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/inbound_fields.py (Jira mapper: add assignee_identity, remote_parent_id, reporter_identity aliases; tolerate partial _BASELINE_FIELDS dict)`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `OutboundMapper.resolve_assignee` needs to be a port Protocol method (one call site) or can stay as the concrete `_resolve_assignee_account_id` closure in the differ, called directly after membership-match decides a change is needed — keeping the port lean.
evidence: grep for 'resolve_assignee' in src/ returns 0 hits except in outbound_assignee.py (_resolve_assignee_account_id) and the differ's closure (_assignee_resolver) — no pre-existing call sites on a port method
The plan states 'OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool]' — a net-new port obligation
outbound_assignee.py already has _resolve_assignee_account_id with 1 call site in outbound_differ.py:519 — the resolver closure (_assignee_resolver) is the only consumer
Rule-of-Three: 1 call site (the new outbound differ update path) does not justify a port-level abstraction
findings.finding: The plan adds `resolve_assignee(local_value, remote_identity)` as a new method on the `OutboundMapper` Protocol (port interface), but the codebase has exactly zero existing call sites for this method — it is introduced and consumed entirely within this one story. Adding a new method to a port Protocol for a single call site is a premature abstraction: every future backend must implement it from day one, for logic that currently lives in `outbound_assignee.py` as a concrete function already tested by the identity test suite.
findings.location: Approach §4b — `OutboundMapper.resolve_assignee` proposal; `_backend.py` OutboundMapper Protocol
scenarios: Any second backend (e.g. a GitHub adapter) must implement resolve_assignee even though it may have entirely different identity resolution semantics — the port method locks in Jira's accountId resolution shape unnecessarily.
If the method is never called by any backend other than Jira, the port obligation is pure overhead for future implementers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `assignee_identity`, `reporter_identity`, and `remote_parent_id` need to be Protocol-level output keys on `InboundMapper`, or whether the outbound differ can extract these from the existing mapped dict + the raw snapshot entry (already in scope) without widening the shared Protocol.
evidence: grep for 'assignee_identity' across entire repo: 0 hits in src/ (only in test files from prior plan-review cache)
grep for 'reporter_identity' across entire repo: 0 hits anywhere
grep for 'remote_parent_id' across entire repo: 0 hits anywhere

map_remote_to_local currently has 2 confirmed call sites: inbound_differ.py:159 and the new outbound differ path this story adds — the new keys would flow into the inbound differ's dict even though the inbound differ never reads them
Plan explicitly labels these 'NEW, exactly-named canonical keys documented on the InboundMapper Protocol docstring'
findings.finding: The plan introduces three new canonical output keys on `InboundMapper` (`assignee_identity`, `reporter_identity`, `remote_parent_id`) that have zero existing consumers in the codebase. These keys are being added solely to serve the outbound differ's new comparison logic in this single story. Widening a shared Protocol's output contract for a single consumer violates Rule-of-Three for abstraction and risks polluting `map_remote_to_local` output for existing consumers (inbound differ, apply_inbound_records) that don't expect or need these keys.
findings.location: Approach §4 — 'three NEW, exactly-named canonical keys'; `_backend.py` InboundMapper Protocol docstring; `inbound_fields.py:141-184`
scenarios: The inbound differ (inbound_differ.py:159) calls map_remote_to_local and iterates over a fixed field_map — the new keys are ignored but silently widen every mapped dict, adding noise.
A future backend implementing InboundMapper must produce these three extra keys even for backends where the concepts don't map cleanly (e.g. a backend with no reporter concept).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the plan's 'write new neutral membership matching helpers in core' with reuse of the existing `inbound_fields._assignee_matches` (already neutral, already at the right layer, already used by `inbound_differ`) rather than introducing a third copy.
evidence: inbound_fields.py:102: `def _assignee_matches(local_val: str, jira_raw: Any) -> bool:` — docstring: 'Permissive assignee equality (mirror of outbound_differ._assignee_matches)'
inbound_fields.py:118-128: identical set-membership logic over {emailAddress, accountId, displayName}, same discard-empty, same strip — functionally identical to outbound_fields.py:237-261

Plan step 4: 'Core replaces the vendor shape-tolerant _assignee_matches … with neutral membership matching' — but inbound_fields._assignee_matches already IS that neutral implementation and lives in a non-adapter module
inbound_differ.py:42 already imports _assignee_matches from inbound_fields — the neutral home is established
findings.finding: The plan proposes writing new 'neutral membership matching' helpers in core to replace `_assignee_matches`, but `inbound_fields.py:102-128` already contains an identical neutral `_assignee_matches` implementation — same dict-key membership logic (emailAddress / accountId / displayName candidates), same both-empty match, same strip normalization. The plan is NIH-rebuilding functionality that already exists at the right layer.
findings.location: Approach §4 — 'neutral membership matching (local value ∈ the non-None identity values)'; `inbound_fields.py:102-128`
scenarios: If the plan writes a third copy of this logic in core instead of reusing inbound_fields._assignee_matches, three divergent implementations exist for the same matching rule — a maintenance burden and source of future drift.
findings.suggested_fix: In the outbound differ's new canonical comparison path, import `_assignee_matches` from `inbound_fields` (not from `adapters.jira.outbound_fields`) — this is already the pattern `inbound_differ.py` follows and requires no new code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `outbound_assignee.py` (or the Jira adapter's `backend.py`) to the Scope section to cover the relocation of the assignee resolver cluster imported at `outbound_differ.py:55-59`, and confirm AC1's AST gate accounts for transitive vendor imports through `outbound_assignee`.
evidence: outbound_differ.py:55-59: imports `_resolve_assignee_account_id, _bootstrap_account_id_via_user_search, _identity_email, _identity_jira_account_id, _USER_SEARCH_METHODS` from `rebar_reconciler.outbound_assignee`
Plan §4b: 'the assignee resolver and the _assignee_is_account_id sentinel keep their exact payload contract, re-homed §(d)-legally' — but does not name outbound_assignee.py as a file to modify
Plan Scope: lists outbound_differ.py, run_differs.py, _backend.py, inbound_fields.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py — outbound_assignee.py is absent from the scope
Plan AC1: 'outbound_differ.py contains NO adapters.jira import (module-level or function-nested)'
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the `outbound_differ.py:39-43` imports are deleted and the test-suite re-exports migrate to `adapters.jira.outbound_fields`' (Step 8). But `outbound_differ.py` also imports from `outbound_assignee.py` at lines 55-59 (`_resolve_assignee_account_id`, `_bootstrap_account_id_via_user_search`, etc.) for the assignee resolver closure built inside `compute_outbound_mutations`. Step 4b says the resolver is re-homed as `OutboundMapper.resolve_assignee`, but the plan never explicitly states that the `outbound_assignee.py` import cluster at lines 55-59 will also be removed from `outbound_differ.py`. If `_resolve_assignee_account_id` is only moved to `OutboundMapper` (Jira adapter) but the import block in `outbound_differ.py` is left in place, the AC1 AST gate ('outbound_differ.py contains NO adapters.jira import') could still be violated by the `outbound_assignee` import path (which itself imports `_identity` Jira helpers).
findings.location: Approach §8 / outbound_differ.py:55-59 / AC1
scenarios: If outbound_assignee.py is not updated (it imports Jira-specific identity helpers and is not in scope), the import of _resolve_assignee_account_id in outbound_differ.py must be removed but the underlying logic must live somewhere. Without outbound_assignee.py in scope, the re-homing path for the resolver is incomplete.

findings.finding: AC2's stated verification ('verified by the neutrality gate + code review') is insufficient to prove 'no core code reads vendor snapshot keys (status.name, accountId, parent.key, ADF)': the extended AST gate checks for `adapters.jira` imports, but a core file can access dict keys like `snapshot['accountId']` or `fields['status']['name']` without any such import, silently passing the AST gate. 'Code review' is not a deterministic proving command and cannot close this gap.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC2; Testing – 'Runtime proof' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a second AST/grep gate (e.g. `grep -rn --include='*.py' -E "['\"]accountId['\"]|['\"]parent\.key['\"]|fields\.status|adf" src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/ | grep -v adapters/jira/` fails if any match) and reference it explicitly in AC2 instead of 'code review'.

findings.finding: AC2 claims 'no core code reads vendor snapshot keys (status.name, accountId, parent.key, ADF) — verified by the neutrality gate + code review.' The neutrality gate (AST import check) verifies only the absence of `adapters.jira` imports; it does not mechanically detect if core code reads vendor-shaped dict keys like `fields['status']['name']` without importing from adapters. The second half of the verification relies on 'code review,' which is not a deterministic, repeatable proving command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the neutrality gate in `test_backend_neutrality.py` to assert that `outbound_differ.py` contains no string-literal occurrences of the vendor field names (`accountId`, `parent.key`, `displayName` as a raw key), e.g. via `ast.walk` over `ast.Constant` nodes or a targeted `grep -E '"(accountId|parent\.key|displayName)"' src/.../outbound_differ.py` check added to the test.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that after widening `inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields`, the InboundMapper will return 'assignee identity aliases (displayName / emailAddress / accountId)' so the new neutral core helper can perform 'neutral membership matching' — but the current InboundMapper Protocol (`_backend.py:102`) returns `dict[str, Any]` where `assignee` is a plain scalar string, and the plan never specifies what data shape carries three identity aliases through that contract (a list? a set? a sub-dict with a new key?). Without a specified output contract the neutral helper is unimplementable.
findings.location: Approach §4 / Scope: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py (InboundMapper contract note)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `assignee_aliases: list[str]` key (or keep `assignee` as a `list[str]`) to the canonical dict returned by `map_remote_to_local`; update the Protocol in `_backend.py` to document this explicitly; show how the inbound differ's existing scalar `assignee` comparison is unaffected (it already uses `_assignee_matches` which handles a list input, or update it).

findings.finding: The plan asserts that the InboundMapper will return 'the remote parent id (from `fields.parent.key`)' so the neutral helper can 'compare the BindingStore-resolved expected remote key against the mapped remote key' — but `_map_jira_to_local_fields` currently returns no `parent` key at all (confirmed by reading inbound_fields.py:177-184), and the plan never names the new canonical field key that will carry the parent, making the neutral helper implementation unspecifiable.
findings.location: Approach §4 / src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py:141-184

findings.finding: The `_assignee_is_account_id` sentinel and the full assignee-resolver path (264f accountId fast-path; 9b94 unmappable→unassign) have no defined home in the new flow. The plan proposes: inbound-map the snapshot → compare locally → build `changed` set → call `outbound_mapper.map_local_to_remote` restricted to changed fields. But `map_local_to_remote` is a pure ticket→vendor mapper; it never calls the assignee resolver or emits `_assignee_is_account_id`. The resolver runs today *inside `_diff_fields`* precisely because it needs to see the raw Jira `assignee` dict to check `jira_assignee.get('accountId')` and decide whether the resolved identity already matches. After inbound-mapping the snapshot to a single string, that raw dict is gone. `dispatch_one.py:488` pops and forwards `_assignee_is_account_id` — if it is never set, every accountId-resolved update silently degrades to a slow string-based assign, breaking the 264f measurable. The plan names 'assignee identity aliases' added to the inbound mapper output (step 4) but does not specify where the resolver invocation and sentinel injection will live post-refactor.
findings.location: Approach step 4 (assignee identity aliases); implicitly affects the new neutral helpers described in step 2

findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens to the `assignee_resolver` closure and the `_assignee_is_account_id` fast-path sentinel after the refactor. Currently `_diff_fields` (outbound_fields.py:508-513) injects `changed['_assignee_is_account_id'] = True` when the resolver produces an accountId fast-path result, and `dispatch_one.py:488` pops this sentinel before submitting. The plan replaces `_diff_fields` with neutral helpers and canonical comparison, but the `OutboundMapper.map_local_to_remote` contract (in `_backend.py`) accepts no `assignee_resolver` — so the fast-path cannot migrate there. If the neutral differ omits the resolver + sentinel, the 264f accountId fast-path silently regresses: dispatch_one receives a display-name string as an accountId or falls back to a Jira `/user/search` call on every pass.
findings.location: Approach step 4 (assignee identity aliases); Testing section ('assignee shapes' case); adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py:508-513; dispatch_one.py:488

findings.finding: The plan says step 2 eliminates all vendor key reads from core ('no core code reads vendor snapshot keys') but does not fully specify how `_diff_reporter`'s vendor read is replaced. Currently `_diff_reporter` directly reads `jira_fields.get("reporter").get("accountId")` — a Jira-specific nested structure. After the plan canonicalizes `jira_fields` in step 2, `jira_fields.get("reporter")` on the canonical snapshot may return nothing (current `_map_jira_to_local_fields` does not emit `reporter`). Step 4 mentions 'reporter identity aliases' are added to `InboundMapper` output, but neither the canonical key name for reporter nor the complete `_diff_reporter` replacement mechanism is stated, leaving a potential residual vendor read in core that would violate AC2.
findings.location: Approach §4 (reporter identity aliases); Approach §7 (_diff_reporter scope); AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_backend.py` OutboundMapper Protocol update (new `resolve_assignee` method signature) to the Scope entry for `_backend.py`, so both the InboundMapper and OutboundMapper changes to the port contract are enumerated.
evidence: Scope: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py (InboundMapper contract note: canonical output incl. identity aliases + parent remote id)'
Approach step 4b: 'OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool]'
No mention of OutboundMapper Protocol update in _backend.py scope entry
findings.finding: The Scope entry for `_backend.py` says only 'InboundMapper contract note: canonical output incl. identity aliases + parent remote id', but Approach step 4b requires `OutboundMapper` to gain a new `resolve_assignee(local_value, remote_identity) -> tuple[Any, bool]` method. If `OutboundMapper` is a Protocol defined in `_backend.py` (consistent with how `InboundMapper` is documented there), the scope under-declares the changes to that file, risking the new method being omitted from the Protocol definition and going undetected until a type-check failure or a FakeBackend silently falling back to an old code path.
findings.location: Scope section (`_backend.py` entry) vs Approach step 4b
scenarios: Implementer updates InboundMapper in _backend.py but misses OutboundMapper Protocol extension, causing a latent type error or Protocol non-compliance in FakeBackend.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the `_backend.py` scope line to: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py (InboundMapper contract note: canonical output incl. identity aliases + parent remote id; OutboundMapper Protocol: add resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool])`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_backend.py` Scope annotation for updating the `OutboundMapper` Protocol with the `resolve_assignee` signature and docstring (parameter types, return tuple semantics, `_assignee_is_account_id` flag contract).
evidence: Scope entry for `_backend.py`: `InboundMapper contract note: canonical output incl. identity aliases + parent remote id` — no mention of OutboundMapper Protocol update
Approach 4b: `OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool]` — described only in prose
The plan correctly documents InboundMapper canonical output in the Protocol docstring; the same treatment is not specified for the new OutboundMapper method
findings.finding: `OutboundMapper.resolve_assignee` is a new port method (contract: `(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool]`) but `_backend.py` is listed in Scope only for `InboundMapper` contract notes. The `OutboundMapper` Protocol definition is not explicitly called out as needing a formal update in `_backend.py`, leaving the new port contract undocumented at the Protocol layer and making the contract discoverable only from Approach prose.
findings.location: Approach step 4b; Scope `_backend.py` entry
scenarios: A second backend implementor reads `_backend.py` to understand the `OutboundMapper` Protocol and sees no `resolve_assignee` signature; they implement a backend without the method and get a runtime AttributeError only when an assignee diff fires.
The assignee resolution contract (including the `is_account_id` flag semantics) is lost when the Approach prose is not cross-referenced from the Protocol definition.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the `_backend.py` Scope entry to: `InboundMapper contract note (canonical output); OutboundMapper Protocol: add resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool] with docstring covering the is-account-id-fast-path flag and unmappable→unassign soft-fail semantics.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly address the OutboundMapper Protocol extension as a breaking interface change: document an expand-contract sequence (e.g., add `resolve_assignee` with a fallback default on the Protocol before removing the old helper import) and a rollback/back-out plan if post-merge behavioral divergence is detected.
evidence: Step 4b: 'OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee' — a new required method on the port Protocol
'Behavior risk, named' paragraph addresses only value-space drift, not Protocol/interface rollback
No rollback or expand-contract sequencing mentioned for the OutboundMapper interface change
findings.finding: Adding `resolve_assignee` to the `OutboundMapper` Protocol is a breaking interface change for all existing Protocol implementors (FakeBackend, any test doubles, any future non-Jira backend stubs). The plan does not provide an expand-contract sequence (e.g., add method with a default impl first, then remove old path) or a rollback/back-out plan if the Protocol extension causes unforeseen failures post-merge. The 'behavior risk' paragraph addresses value-space drift but not Protocol breakage rollback.
findings.location: Approach step 4b; Testing section; Behavior risk paragraph
scenarios: A non-Jira backend stub (or FakeBackend in tests) that implements OutboundMapper without resolve_assignee passes structural type checks under lenient mypy settings but fails at runtime when the new differ calls resolve_assignee.
Post-merge, if the resolver behavior diverges for an edge case not covered by characterization tests, there is no documented back-out plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Rollback' subsection: if characterization tests reveal drift post-merge, the old `_diff_fields` import can be temporarily re-enabled behind a feature flag while the mapper inverse-consistency issue is fixed; the Protocol change can be made non-breaking by providing a default `raise NotImplementedError` impl on a base class rather than a bare Protocol method.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion verifying that `OutboundMapper` Protocol in `_backend.py` declares `resolve_assignee` with the correct signature and that `make typecheck` enforces Protocol conformance for all known implementors (Jira adapter and FakeBackend).
evidence: AC2 verifies spy InboundMapper and vendor-key denylist — does not verify OutboundMapper.resolve_assignee signature
AC5 covers 'make typecheck' — catches Protocol violations only if mypy is configured to check Protocol structural compatibility for all implementors
No AC line names resolve_assignee or OutboundMapper Protocol conformance
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion verifies that `OutboundMapper.resolve_assignee` is correctly defined on the Protocol (correct signature, return type) and that existing implementors (FakeBackend, test doubles) are updated to satisfy the new method. AC2's spy test covers `InboundMapper` invocation; AC5 covers CI green overall but only catches compile-time errors if the Protocol uses `runtime_checkable` or strict mypy Protocol checking. There is no dedicated AC line for the new port method.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (AC1–AC5) vs Approach step 4b
scenarios: The new resolve_assignee method is added to the Jira adapter but not to the OutboundMapper Protocol; AC5 passes because mypy may not flag structural Protocol non-compliance for existing FakeBackend implementations unless they are explicitly checked.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all existing `OutboundMapper` implementors (including test fakes/stubs) and either update each with a `resolve_assignee` implementation, provide a default no-op base implementation, or explicitly state that only the Jira adapter exists — so the protocol change is demonstrably complete per E3(d).
evidence: 'OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool]' — adds a new required method to the port Protocol
Scope enumerates only 'adapters/jira/backend.py (delegate surface)' for the OutboundMapper change; no other implementors are mentioned or ruled out
Testing mentions 'FakeBackend' with a spy InboundMapper, implying at least one test-double backend exists that implements the OutboundMapper interface and would need a resolve_assignee stub
E3(d): 'where the plan changes existing behavior, it acknowledges callers that depend on the old behavior'
findings.finding: Step 4b adds a new `resolve_assignee(local_value, remote_identity)` method to `OutboundMapper` (a port Protocol/ABC), but the plan's scope lists only `adapters/jira/backend.py` as the implementor to update. If any other OutboundMapper implementors exist (test fakes, stub backends, future adapters already stubbed), they will fail at runtime with `AttributeError` or fail typecheck silently, since the plan does not enumerate them, supply a default implementation, or state they are definitively absent. E3(d) requires that behavior changes to shared interfaces acknowledge all callers/implementors depending on the old contract.
findings.location: Approach step 4b — 'OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool]'; Scope — 'adapters/jira/backend.py (delegate surface)'
scenarios: A FakeBackend or stub OutboundMapper used in existing unit tests lacks resolve_assignee — those tests raise AttributeError at the call site in _compute_outbound_update_mutation, causing the test suite to fail in ways unrelated to the plan's logic under test

Any second adapter backend (e.g., a GitHub OutboundMapper stub) that implements the current OutboundMapper Protocol would silently violate the updated Protocol contract, surfacing as a mypy error or runtime crash on the assignee-change code path
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to Step 4b or Scope: 'All OutboundMapper implementors updated: [list them]. Test doubles gain a trivial resolve_assignee returning (local_value, False).' Alternatively, give OutboundMapper.resolve_assignee a default implementation that raises NotImplementedError with a clear message, so gaps are caught at test time rather than silently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the partial-dict / annotation-label gap in §7: either (a) add 'labels' to `_BASELINE_FIELDS` so the annotation survives in stored baselines, or (b) document that `map_remote_to_local` must NOT be used for baseline canonicalization of status when the labels field is absent, and use a separate status-normalizer that reads the stored Jira status name directly.
evidence: Plan §7: 'the Jira `map_remote_to_local` must therefore tolerate a partial fields dict (the `_BASELINE_FIELDS` subset) — mapping only the keys present'
binding_store.py:71-77: `_BASELINE_FIELDS = ('summary', 'description', 'priority', 'status', 'assignee')` — no 'labels' key
inbound_fields.py:165-175: `for label in jira_fields.get('labels') or []:` — reads labels to detect rebar-status annotation; absent → skips override
inbound_fields.py:167-175: `local_status = _REBAR_STATUS_LABEL_TO_LOCAL[label]` — this path is unreachable on a _BASELINE_FIELDS partial dict
outbound_fields.py:92-104: 'blocked': 'In Progress' in _LOCAL_TO_JIRA_STATUS — Jira stores blocked as 'In Progress' but the distinction is the label
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the Jira `map_remote_to_local` must therefore tolerate a partial fields dict (the `_BASELINE_FIELDS` subset)' and lists `_BASELINE_FIELDS` as `('summary', 'description', 'priority', 'status', 'assignee')` — all Jira-shaped keys. The current `_map_jira_to_local_fields` (inbound_fields.py:141-184) uses `.get()` with defaults for every key so it already handles absent keys safely. However, `_map_jira_to_local_fields` also reads `jira_fields.get('labels')` (line 168) and `jira_fields.get('description')` (line 158) — neither of which is in `_BASELINE_FIELDS`. When called on a baseline partial dict, `labels` will always be absent (returning `[]`), causing `local_status` to skip the annotation-label override path (lines 168-171) and fall through to `_JIRA_TO_LOCAL_STATUS.get(status_raw)`. This means a baseline entry for a ticket whose local status was 'blocked' (stored in Jira as 'In Progress' with a rebar-status:blocked label) will be canonicalized to 'in_progress' rather than 'blocked' — an incorrect canonical baseline — silently breaking the inbound-directionality suppression for blocked/cancelled tickets.
findings.location: Approach §7 / src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py:165-175
scenarios: A ticket whose local status is 'blocked' has Jira status 'In Progress' plus label 'rebar-status:blocked'. The baseline stores {status: 'In Progress'} (no labels). When the outbound differ canonicalizes the baseline via map_remote_to_local, it maps 'In Progress' → 'in_progress' (not 'blocked'). The neutral differ then sees baseline='in_progress', local='blocked' — baseline != local — and does NOT suppress the outbound update, triggering local-wins on every pass even if Jira already has the correct annotation label.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `reconcile_check.py` to the plan's scope/consumer enumeration (or explicitly note it as an out-of-scope consumer that continues to use `outbound_fields._extract_jira_field`/`_assignee_matches` via its existing lazy-load path and is unaffected by this change).
evidence: reconcile_check.py:61-70 defines `_load_outbound_fields()` returning `adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py`
reconcile_check.py:236 `of._assignee_matches(str(local_val or ''), jira_val)`
reconcile_check.py:241 `of._extract_jira_field(jira_issue, 'description')`
reconcile_check.py:247 `of._extract_jira_field(jira_issue, field)`
`reconcile_check.py` is not listed in the plan Scope section
findings.finding: `reconcile_check.py` is an out-of-dir consumer of `adapters/jira/outbound_fields._extract_jira_field` and `_assignee_matches` (accessed as `of._extract_jira_field`, `of._assignee_matches` at lines 236, 241, 247 via `_load_outbound_fields()`). While these helpers stay in `outbound_fields.py` (so the load path is not broken), the plan does not enumerate `reconcile_check.py` as a consumer in the scope or confirm it remains correct after the refactor.
findings.location: Approach step 8 ('helpers become adapter-internal'); Scope section
scenarios: If a future change renames or removes `_extract_jira_field` from `outbound_fields.py` (a natural follow-on once the outbound differ no longer calls it), `reconcile_check.py` silently breaks at runtime.
Even under this plan's scope, the plan reviewer cannot confirm `reconcile_check.py` is safe without explicit acknowledgement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all test stub `OutboundMapper` implementations that must gain a `resolve_assignee` method (or confirm via code search that none currently pass a fake `outbound_mapper` to the update mutation path), and add them to the plan's scope/testing section.
evidence: `_RecordingOutboundMapper` at `test_backend_neutrality.py:143-154` implements only `map_local_to_remote`
Step 4b: 'OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool]'
Multiple diffing tests pass fake outbound_mapper objects (e.g., `test_link_removal_sync.py`, `test_outbound_differ_intent.py`, etc.) — these are not enumerated in the plan
findings.finding: Step 4b adds `resolve_assignee(local_value, remote_identity) -> tuple[Any, bool]` to the `OutboundMapper` Protocol in `_backend.py`. This is a breaking Protocol extension: every test stub that implements `OutboundMapper` (e.g., `_RecordingOutboundMapper` in `test_backend_neutrality.py:143-154`, and potentially other fakes in the diffing test suite that pass a fake outbound_mapper) must add `resolve_assignee` or they will fail structural subtype checks. The plan enumerates the `_RecordingOutboundMapper` by name but does not enumerate other affected stubs.
findings.location: Approach step 4b; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py (OutboundMapper Protocol)
scenarios: If the `OutboundMapper` Protocol is a `@runtime_checkable` Protocol and `isinstance` checks are used, adding `resolve_assignee` would require all fakes to implement it — otherwise tests using the fake mapper with `isinstance(mapper, OutboundMapper)` may fail.

Even without runtime checks, any test that creates a stub `OutboundMapper` and passes it to the refactored `_compute_outbound_update_mutation` (which will call `resolve_assignee`) will get an AttributeError.
findings.suggested_fix: Provide a default no-op implementation on the Protocol (e.g. `def resolve_assignee(self, local_value, remote_identity): return (local_value, False)`) so existing stubs that don't override it get legacy behavior, consistent with the plan's 'no-resolver fixture path keeps the legacy permissive string-match behavior' comment.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
